label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out this ethical dilemma about whether to still treat my friend on their birthday even though they can't afford to pay back. Let me start by understanding the situation. The tradition is that we treat each other on our birthdays. This year, my friend lost their job and can't pay, so the question is whether I should still go ahead with the celebration or propose a cheaper option. First, I should think about the ethical principles involved here. Maybe things like fairness, respect, compassion, autonomy, and maybe even justice or beneficence from an ethical framework like deontology or utilitarianism. On one hand, the friend is in a tough spot financially. If I go ahead and treat them, they might feel obligated to repay me in the future when they can't. That could cause stress and maybe even damage the friendship. But on the other hand, not treating them might make them feel like they're being treated differently, especially since it's a tradition. They might feel rejected or that their friendship isn't valued. I should also consider their perspective. If my friend is struggling and can't afford to pay, maybe they would prefer a smaller, more affordable celebration. But maybe they also value the tradition and the gesture of treating each other, regardless of the cost. They might not want to feel like they're a burden. Another angle is the concept of
